revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Kamo Shrine]]

revision.comment: initial text of a start of an article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction?
revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} '''Kamigamo Shrine''' is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.
Kamigamo jinja is amongst the oldest in Japan and it is featured prominently amongst "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]" which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]].<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan, including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-706-81632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-706-81632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&dq=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655]
==External links== * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html Kamigamo Shrine website]

(1959). revision.comment: /* External links */ + ja:賀茂別雷神社
revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] '''Kamigamo Shrine''' is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.
Kamigamo jinja is amongst the oldest in Japan and it is featured prominently amongst "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]" which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]].<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-706-81632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-706-81632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82259-0 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&dq=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655]
==External links== * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html Kamigamo Shrine website]

Kamigamo jinja is amongst the oldest in Japan and it is featured prominently amongst "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]" which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]].<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.

In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>

[[ja:賀茂別雷神社]]

revision.comment: woods = vestiges of Tadasu no Mori
revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Kamigamo-1559.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Complex architectural elements are somewhat enhanced by the bright color.]] {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.
It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto. The Kamo jinja serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-706-81632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-706-81632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82259-0 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&dq=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655]
==External links== * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html Kamigamo Shrine website]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). {{Commonscat|Kamigamo-jinja|Kamigamo-jinja}}
{{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}}
[[Category:7th-century architecture]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]]
[[ja:賀茂別雷神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Kamigamo-1559.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Complex architectural elements are somewhat enhanced by the bright color.]] {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175.</ref>
It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

[http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-706-81632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-706-81632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82259-0 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&dq=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}}

{{Commonscat|Kamigamo-jinja|Kamigamo-jinja}}
{{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}}
[[Category:7th-century architecture]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]]
[[ja:賀茂別雷神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Kamigamo-1559.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Complex architectural elements are somewhat enhanced by the bright color.]] {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175.</ref>
It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor Yenyū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|vestige of primeval forest </gallery>

==Structures== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.

{{Commonscat|Kamigamo-jinja|Kamigamo-jinja}}
{{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}}
[[Category:7th-century architecture]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]]
[[ja:賀茂別雷神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Kamigamo-1559.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Complex architectural elements are somewhat enhanced by the bright color.]] {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175.</ref>
It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131.</ref> * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakimikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132.</ref>
==Structures== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|vestige of primeval forest </gallery>

Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131.</ref> * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132.</ref>
==Structures== The jinja is famous for its Haiden (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.

[[ja:賀茂別雷神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Kamigamo-1559.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Complex architectural elements are somewhat enhanced by the bright color.]] {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto, built in 678.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175.</ref>
It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131.</ref> * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132.</ref>
==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Kamigamo-1559.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Complex architectural elements are somewhat enhanced by the bright color.]] {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto, built in 678.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175.</ref>
It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder.
==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206.</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131.</ref> * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132.</ref>
==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6'').
A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|vestige of primeval forest </gallery>

[http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}}

[[ko:가모와케이카즈치 신사]] [[hu:Kamigamo szentély]] [[ja:賀茂別雷神社]] [[pt:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[sv:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[zh:賀茂別雷神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | width = | caption = | type = | dedication = Kamowakeikaduchinomikoto | founded = 678 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture‎]] | phone = 075-781-0011‎ | website = http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto, built in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]　The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|Romon File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. {{Main|Kamo Shrine}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | width = | caption = | type = | dedication = Kamowakeikaduchinomikoto | founded = 678 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture‎]] | phone = 075-781-0011‎ | website = http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto, built in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]　The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} ==External links== * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Commonscat|Kamigamo-jinja|Kamigamo-jinja}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:7th-century architecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[ko:가모와케이카즈치 신사]] [[hu:Kamigamo szentély]] [[ja:賀茂別雷神社]] [[pt:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[sv:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[zh:賀茂別雷神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | width = | caption = | type = | dedication = Kamowakeikaduchinomikoto | founded = 678 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture‎]] | phone = 075-781-0011‎ | website = http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at ''Kamigamo-jinja.'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Konin]] (770-781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Kamigamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-7068-1632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2259-0 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&dq=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} ==External links== * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Commonscat|Kamigamo-jinja|Kamigamo-jinja}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:7th-century architecture]] [[Category:678 establishments]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[ko:가모와케이카즈치 신사]] [[hu:Kamigamo szentély]] [[ja:賀茂別雷神社]] [[pt:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[sv:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[zh:賀茂別雷神社]]

revision.comment: changed "northest Kyoto" to "north Kyoto". It's not really in the east (e.g., it's slightly west of the old imperial palace).

Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]].
