''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023181947/http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html |date=2017-10-23 }} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:confused probably misplaced articles linked from page Torinoichi probably under wrong names recheck that page and its outlinks later]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] {{Kamo Faith}}

{{Short description|Shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shikinaisha]]
[[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamowakeikazuchinomikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023181947/http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html |date=2017-10-23 }} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:confused probably misplaced articles linked from page Torinoichi probably under wrong names recheck that page and its outlinks later]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] {{Kamo Faith}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023181947/http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html |date=2017-10-23 }} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:confused probably misplaced articles linked from page Torinoichi probably under wrong names recheck that page and its outlinks later]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] {{Kamo Faith}}

{{Short description|Shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shikinaisha]]
[[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamowakeikazuchinomikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023181947/http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html |date=2017-10-23 }} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:confused probably misplaced articles linked from page Torinoichi probably under wrong names recheck that page and its outlinks later]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] {{Kamo Faith}}

[http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023181947/http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html |date=2017-10-23 }} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 678]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamigamo Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:confused probably misplaced articles linked from page Torinoichi probably under wrong names recheck that page and its outlinks later]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] {{Kamo Faith}}

[[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamowakeikazuchinomikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. 上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shikinaisha]]

[[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamowakeikazuchinomikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Short description|Shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shikinaisha]]
[[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamowakeikazuchinomikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Short description|Shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shikinaisha]]
[[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamowakeikazuchinomikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Short description|Shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = Kamigamo-1559.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shikinaisha]]
[[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamowakeikazuchinomikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[Hōei]] 8''): [[Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society.
