(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023181947/http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html |date=2017-10-23 }} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] }} [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:معبد كاميغامو]] [[arz:مزار كامى جامو]] [[ca:Santuari de Kamigamo]] {{Commons category|Kamigamo-jinja}} [[el:Ναός Καμιγκάμο]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kamigamo]] [[es:Santuario Kamigamo]] [[fa:معبد کامیگامو]] [[fi:Kamigamo-jinja]] [[fr:Kamigamo-jinja]] [[hu:Kamigamo szentély]] [[id:Kuil Kamigamo]] [[ja:賀茂別雷神社]] [[ko:가모와케이카즈치 신사]] [[la:Templum Kamigamo]] [[ne:कामिगामो तीर्थ]] [[pl:Kamigamo-jinja]] [[pt:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[ru:Камигамо-дзиндзя]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kamigamo Shrine]] [[sl:Svetišče Kamigamo]] [[sv:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าคามิงาโมะ]] [[tr:Kamigamo Tapınağı]] [[zh:賀茂別雷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q700448}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

上賀茂神社 | image = File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja24n3200.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.06027778|N|135.75277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Shikinaisha]]
[[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|WD=Q11634943}} | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|WD=Q11634943}} | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] [[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|WD=Q11634943}} | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|WD=Q11634943}} | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q700448]] Wikidata name: Kamigamo Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamigamo Shrine
上賀茂神社 | image = File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja24n3200.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.06027778|N|135.75277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Engishiki#Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Shikinaisha]]
[[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|WD=Q11634943}} | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category|Kamigamo-jinja}} * [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html Kamigamo Shrine website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023181947/http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index.html |date=2017-10-23 }} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Kamigamo Shrine]] [[ar:معبد كاميغامو]] [[arz:مزار كامى جامو]] [[ca:Santuari de Kamigamo]] {{Commons category|Kamigamo-jinja}} [[el:Ναός Καμιγκάμο]] [[eo:Sanktejo Kamigamo]] [[es:Santuario Kamigamo]] [[fa:معبد کامیگامو]] [[fi:Kamigamo-jinja]] [[fr:Kamigamo-jinja]] [[hu:Kamigamo szentély]] [[id:Kuil Kamigamo]] [[ja:賀茂別雷神社]] [[ko:가모와케이카즈치 신사]] [[la:Templum Kamigamo]] [[ne:कामिगामो तीर्थ]] [[pl:Kamigamo-jinja]] [[pt:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[ru:Камигамо-дзиндзя]] [[simple:Kamigamo Shrine]] [[sl:Svetišče Kamigamo]] [[sv:Kamowakeikazuchi-jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าคามิงาโมะ]] [[tr:Kamigamo Tapınağı]] [[zh:賀茂別雷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q700448}} [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:7th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]]

[[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]]
''Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya''
Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo jinja]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|WD=Q11634943}} | founded_by = | established = 678 | date_destroyed = | location = 339 Kamigamomotoyama, [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.kamigamojinja.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = Sangensya-[[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Kita-ku|WD=Q1154970}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kamigamo Shrine'''|上賀茂神社|''Kamigamo Jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in north Kyoto, first founded in 678.Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ Kamigamo] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090828050742/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/01/ |date=2009-08-28 }}; Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=clan+kyogoku&pg=PA1704 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.] Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|'''Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine'''|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 119-175. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and one of the seventeen [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] as a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to [[Shimogamo Shrine]] and Kamigamo Shrine, the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines]] of Kyoto.Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Kamogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Ajisukitakahikone|Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]], the ''kami'' of thunder. ==History== [[File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja31ss4272.jpg|thumb|right|A serene expanse at the shrine]] [[File:Karasu-zumo a.jpg|thumb|Karasu-zumo - lit. "crow sumo", a part of the festivities held each year at the shrine during [[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Choyo]].'']] The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Records from the reign of [[:en:Emperor Heizei|Emperor Heizei]] (806-809) mention that ''Kamo-mioya jinja'' was amongst a select number of establishments which had been granted a divine seal for use on documents. The seal would have been enshrined in its own unique ''[[mikoshi]]'' (''Oshite jinja''). This granting of a special seal and the practices associated with its use and preservation conformed to a pattern established by [[:en:Emperor Kōnin|Emperor Kōnin]] (770–781) in 778 (''[[:en:Hōki|Hōki]] 9'').Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto'', p. 206. In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo Wake-ikazuchi.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines'', pp. 116–117. Kamigamo, along with the Shimogamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''", p. 1.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Kamigamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', pp. 47, 131. * '''942''' (''[[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''[[:en:Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[:en:Emperor En'yū|Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', pp. 48, 131. * '''1711''' (''[[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 8''): [[:en:Emperor Nakamikado|Emperor Nakamikado]] took refuge in the Hosodono when the palace had become uninhabitable.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 132. ==Structures== The jinja is famous for its ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall), rebuilt in 1628-1629 (''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] 6''). A number of priests' residences are situated on its grounds, and one, the Nishimura House, is open to the public. ===Gallery=== File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja03n3200.jpg|Geheiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja09n4272.jpg|Nara-no-ogawa File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja05n3200.jpg|Kita-shinsenjo File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja12n4272.jpg|Tatesuna & Saiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja17n3200.jpg|Hashiden File:Kamo-wakeikazuchi-jinja18n4272.jpg|[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]] File:Kamigamo-1569.jpg|Vestige of primeval forest ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[:en:Yurihonjo hinakaido|Yurihonjo hinakaido]], an annual traditional doll display festival held in part of the shrine ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]].
